nativeRange.habitat: Upper reef slopes and protected lagoons in tropical Indo-Pacific waters, where colonies are typically attached to hard reef substrate rather than free-living on soft sediment.

decorPreferences: stable flat surface for the encrusting base
open area with clearance from neighboring corals

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Has been documented benefiting from weekly target feeding of small meaty foods — mysis shrimp, finely chopped krill, brine shrimp, or commercial LPS pellet/powder mixes. Feeding tentacles generally extend in the evening or after dark; turning off circulation pumps for 15–30 minutes during feeding tends to improve uptake. Overfeeding has been reported to cause tissue distension, so once-weekly is a reasonable cadence.

goodWith: non-aggressive LPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: aggressive LPS such as Galaxea, Hammer, or Torch without spacing
bulldozing tankmates such as large hermit crabs or urchins

imageCandidates.notes: By Oceanamazon; 2901x2364. Vibrant actinic/blue-light aquarium shot of a Symphyllia brain coral showing fluorescent orange/green fused meandering (flabello-meandroid) walls — the exact hobbyist reef-lit look; sharp, whole colony centered. Identified to the Symphyllia trade genus, not to species L. recta.

By Oceanamazon; 3057x2436. Sharp actinic shot of a red-and-green fluorescent Symphyllia brain coral, a different color morph for variety; whole colony centered on black background. Symphyllia trade genus, not confirmed to species.

By Ryan McMinds / Joe Pollock; 2736x3648. Sharp in-situ daylight close-up on the Great Barrier Reef showing the natural tan/green fused brain-like surface — represents the wild appearance. Symphyllia trade genus, not confirmed to species.

summary: Symphyllia Brain Coral is a fleshy large polyp stony (LPS) coral recognizable by its tightly fused walls and meandering, brain-like surface. It is generally considered an intermediate-level coral, doing best under moderate light, gentle indirect flow, and weekly target feeding of small meaty foods.
careNotes:
Symphyllia Brain Coral is the long-standing aquarium trade name for corals that were formerly classified under the genus Symphyllia. Following molecular taxonomic revision, Symphyllia has been subsumed into Lobophyllia, and the accepted scientific name is now Lobophyllia recta (Dana, 1846). The aquarium trade continues to market these corals as 'Symphyllia' — a category recognized by the tightly fused corallite walls that form a meandering, brain-like surface (a structure known as a flabello-meandroid skeleton), which distinguishes them from the more separated, lobed polyps of Lobophyllia hemprichii. Specimens are commonly sold under names such as 'Wilson's Dented Brain', 'Closed Brain', and various designer color morphs including 'Pirate's Plunder Symphyllia' and 'Lemon Berry Symphyllia'. All Symphyllia trade specimens — regardless of color morph or commercial name — share essentially identical husbandry requirements.

Lighting: Generally advised to keep at approximately 60–150 PAR (low to moderate). Sources converge on the low-to-moderate end; some hobby guides suggest starting around 50 PAR and acclimating up to 100–150 PAR over several weeks. Intense SPS-level lighting (>200 PAR) tends to cause bleaching. New specimens are best introduced at the lower end and moved incrementally over 2–4 weeks.

Flow: Low to moderate, indirect water movement is generally recommended. Direct laminar flow has been known to cause tissue retraction; randomized, gentle current is preferred. Flow should be sufficient to clear detritus from the coral's surface without flattening or stressing the tissue.

Placement: Best positioned on the sandbed or lower rockwork on a stable, flat surface. Allow at least 10–15 cm of clear space around the colony on all sides. Symphyllia has been reported to extend long sweeper tentacles, particularly at night, that can sting and damage adjacent corals. As with other Lobophylliidae, mesenteric filaments may also be extended nocturnally to attack neighbors and may go unnoticed by morning.

Feeding: Weekly target feeding of meaty foods is generally recommended to support coloration and tissue health. Mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, finely chopped krill, and commercial LPS foods are well-accepted. Reduce circulation during feeding and feed in the evening when tentacles are extended.

Water quality: Stable parameters are more important than hitting exact targets. Moderate nutrient levels (nitrate 5–15 ppm, phosphate 0.05–0.1 ppm) tend to support best coloration. Ultra-low-nutrient systems can stress the coral. Calcium, alkalinity, and magnesium supplementation (two-part dosing or calcium reactor) is recommended for sustained skeletal growth.

Growth: Slow grower in captivity. Wild colonies have been documented reaching well over a meter across; aquarium specimens typically reach 10–40 cm over years.

Fragmentation: Possible but slow to recover and higher-risk than faster-growing LPS. Not routinely fragged by hobbyists; most trade specimens are wild-collected from Indonesia, Australia, and the Philippines.

Note on aggression range: Sources vary on sweeper tentacle reach; conservative spacing of 10–15 cm (4–6 inches) from neighboring corals is a reasonable default.

Distinction from Lobophyllia hemprichii: Symphyllia and [lobophyllia-coral] are closely related and share essentially identical husbandry. The two are visually distinguishable — Symphyllia has fused corallite walls forming a continuous brain-like surface, while Lobophyllia hemprichii has more separated, lobed polyps — and retailers and hobbyists in the trade continue to differentiate them on this basis.
breedingNotes: Reproduces sexually via broadcast spawning on natural reefs, but captive sexual reproduction is not practiced in the hobby. Fragmentation is technically possible — a bandsaw or bone cutter is typically recommended for clean cuts — but Lobophylliidae are slow-healing and considered higher-risk to frag than many other LPS corals. Aquacultured specimens are available from specialty coral retailers and represent a growing share of the trade, though a significant portion of stock continues to be sourced from wild collection, particularly from Indonesia and Australia.

primary.name: World Wide Corals — Symphyllia Collection
primary.notes: Primary trade source. Symphyllia treated as a distinct trade category from Lobophyllia. Active inventory includes designer color morphs (Pirate's Plunder Symphyllia, Lemon Berry Symphyllia, and ~114 additional Symphyllia search results). Collection page itself does not include numerical husbandry parameters — cross-source verification used for water values.

additional.name: Reeflings — Sinuous Cup Coral (Symphyllia recta) Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-verification secondary. PAR 50–150; temperature 25–28°C (77–82°F); SG 1.023–1.025; pH 8.1–8.4; flow moderate; care level 'moderate to easy' (variation by color morph); native range Indo-Pacific reef slopes and lagoons; family Mussidae (historic — superseded by Lobophylliidae); order Scleractinia. Temperature upper bound averaged with Extreme Corals' 24–27°C to land at 24–27°C.

additional.name: ReefChasers — Symphyllia Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-verification source. Confirms low-to-moderate lighting (start low, acclimate up); moderate water movement; weekly target feeding (mysis/brine shrimp); 'aggressive' designation with long sweeper tentacles capable of stinging neighbors. Family listed as Mussidae (older classification). No specific PAR or numerical parameters provided.

additional.name: Extreme Corals — Symphyllia Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-verification source. Temperature 75–80°F (24–27°C); pH 8.0–8.4; alkalinity 8–11 dKH; flow moderate indirect; placement lower-to-middle on flat stable surface; lighting 'moderate to moderately high' with slow acclimation; feeding small meaty foods weekly. Care level described as manageable in stable reef but more attention than beginner soft corals.

additional.name: WoRMS — Lobophyllia recta (Dana, 1846)
additional.notes: Taxonomic authority. Confirms Symphyllia recta as unaccepted junior synonym; accepted name is Lobophyllia recta. Family Lobophylliidae, order Scleractinia. Distribution: Indo-Pacific (recorded from Indian and Japanese EEZs and broader Indo-West Pacific reefs). Used for taxonomy only; not for husbandry parameters.
